text: We have a plan, and it is about suppliers, and it will save some money over a few years.
Today I propose a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation plan with projected savings of four million dollars.

I am presenting a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal, and the headline figure is four point two million in savings.
Strong opening. Walk me through the rationale -- why eleven suppliers down to four?
The single biggest value driver is volume. The cost-benefit is clear: a payback period under eleven months and a strong return on investment.
Good. The board will push on delivery risk. How do you protect it?
With a staged approach -- a phased rollout designed to minimize disruption, region by region, not a single switch.

Good afternoon, everyone, and thank you for the time. I am presenting a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal, and I will keep it tight. Let me start with the headline figure. The projected savings from the renegotiated freight contracts come to four point two million dollars over the three years. That is the number I want you to remember. Now, the rationale. Today we run eleven overlapping regional suppliers, and the consolidation reduces that to four strategic partners. The single biggest value driver is volume: fewer contracts at higher volume gives us better rates. The cost-benefit is clear. The upfront investment is one point one million, and the payback period is under eleven months, which gives us a return on investment of roughly two hundred and eighty percent across the term. I have tried to quantify every assumption, so the business case is built on real figures, not estimates. To protect delivery, I am proposing a staged approach: a phased rollout designed to minimize disruption, region by region, rather than a single switch. In short, this is a compelling, low-risk path to a leaner supply base, and the numbers underpin every claim. I am happy to take questions on any line of the model.

Hi Fabiana, it is Marcus. I just read through the strategic proposal, and overall it is genuinely compelling. The headline figure landed well with me, and the rationale for moving from eleven suppliers down to four is solid. A couple of things before the leadership review. First, I want the business case to be airtight, so could you quantify the disruption risk during the transition, not just the savings? Right now the cost-benefit leans heavily on the upside. Second, the payback period under eleven months is impressive, but walk me through how you calculated the return on investment, because the board will push on that number. Third, I like the staged approach, but tell me which region is the first phase and why, since that choice is itself a value driver. If the consolidation logic underpins the whole thing the way you describe, this will pass. Send me the updated model and I will book the slot with leadership. Thanks, and nice work on this.

Fabiana opens with the headline figure: four point two million in projected savings. She then gives the rationale, moving from eleven overlapping suppliers down to four strategic partners. She lays out the cost-benefit, showing a payback period under eleven months and a strong return on investment. To protect delivery, she proposes a staged approach, a phased rollout region by region designed to minimize disruption. She closes by saying the consolidation logic underpins every claim, so the business case is compelling and low-risk.
